content.description:
Hobson uses a unified view system where a single "view" concept replaces the earlier renderer/editor distinction. Views can be read-only, editable, or mixed-mode.

## Two Kinds of Views

### 1. View Code (Imperative)

Code items with `type: view` that export a `render(item, api)` function returning DOM:

```javascript
export async function render(item, api) {
  const div = document.createElement('div');
  div.textContent = item.content?.description || '';
  return div;
}
```

The view declares `content.for_type` to specify which item type it renders.

### 2. View Specifications (Declarative)

Data items with `type: view-spec` containing `ui_hints` that describe structure. Consumed by `generic-view`:

```javascript
{
  type: "view-spec-type-uuid",
  content: {
    for_type: "note-type-uuid",
    ui_hints: [
      { field: "name", label: "Title", fieldView: "field-view-text" },
      { field: "content.description", label: "Content", fieldView: "field-view-markdown" }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Field Views

Reusable components that render individual properties. Support multiple modes:

- **readonly** — display value without interaction
- **editable** — allow modification via `onChange` callback

Field views can be unified (one implementation handles both modes) or separate (e.g., `field-view-markdown-readonly` and `field-view-markdown-editable`).

## View Resolution

When displaying an item, the system resolves which view to use through a five-step hierarchy (see [`resolveView()`](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=resolveView)):

1. **Explicit** — `viewId` parameter passed to `renderItem()`
2. **Item preference** — the item itself has a `preferredView` field
3. **Type preference** — the type definition has a `preferredView` field
4. **Type chain** — walk up `extends` chain, find first view with matching `for_type`
5. **Fallback** — [kernel:default-view](item://aaaaaaaa-1111-0000-0000-000000000000) (JSON display)

The parent's attachment `view.type` corresponds to step 1 (explicit viewId). For the full API reference and view discovery methods, see [Views & Rendering overview](item://a0a0a0a0-d0c0-4000-8000-000000000004).

### View Toggling with `previousView`

When switching an attachment's view, the old configuration is preserved in the `previousView` field of the [attachment](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) object, allowing toggling back to restore all previous settings.

## Multiple Views Per Type

A type can have many views. For example, a `note` type might have:
- `note-view-editable` — full editable markdown view
- `note-view-readonly` — rendered markdown, no editing
- `note-view-compact` — title + first line only
- `note-view-card` — card layout for grid display

Users choose between them via context menus, and preferences are remembered.

## The Generic View

The `generic-view` is a universal fallback that:
1. Reads the item's type to find view specs
2. Iterates through `ui_hints`
3. Loads appropriate field views for each property
4. Assembles a complete interface with per-field mode control

## Design Rationale

### Why Unified Views (Not Separate Renderers/Editors)?

The original system had renderers (display) and editors (modify) as separate item types. This was replaced with a single view concept because:
- Most views naturally want to be editable (click a title to edit it)
- The renderer/editor split forced duplicate code
- Per-property mode control (some fields readonly, others editable) is more flexible than all-or-nothing

### Why Separate from Types?

Views are items, not embedded in type definitions. This means:
- Add a new view without modifying the type
- Users can create alternative views
- Views are inspectable and modifiable like everything else

### Why Multiple Allowed?

Different contexts need different presentations. A note in a spatial container might use a compact card view, while the same note navigated to directly uses a full editable view.

## Hob Views and Reactivity (Feb 2026)

Hob views are the preferred way to write new views. They use `content.hob` (compact JSON AST) instead of `content.code` (JS). The rendering system evaluates the Hob code and uses morphdom to diff the result against the existing DOM.

### Dependency Tracking

During Hob view evaluation, the rendering system's `DependencyTracker` records which items and atoms are accessed. When those items change, only affected views re-render. Key mechanisms:

- **Selector-filtered reactivity**: `(get-item id :select fn)` declares which slice of data the view cares about. Re-renders fire only when the selector output changes (compared via `deepEquals`).
- **Selector pins**: Once a view registers a selector for an item, later bare `get-item` calls on the same item don't widen the selector. The selector is the intentional reactivity contract.
- **Bare dep fallback**: Views that don't call `get-item` on their own item get an implicit bare dependency (backward-compatible with simple views).
- **Self-owned atoms**: Atoms created during a view's evaluation don't become dependencies of that view — `reset!` drives local updates without triggering re-renders.

### Nested Rendering

Views can render child items via `render-item`. Each child render instance tracks its own dependencies independently. Parents don't depend on children — editing a child re-renders only that child, not the parent.

### Morphdom Handler Retention

When morphdom patches the DOM after a re-render, it keeps old event handlers instead of transferring new ones from the discarded DOM tree. Old handlers have correct DOM refs to the kept elements. This means Hob event handlers work correctly across re-renders without special handling.

### Hob↔JS Interop Pitfalls

Hob functions are always async (return Promises). Never pass them as synchronous callbacks to JS APIs (`Array.find`, `Array.sort`, etc.) — the Promise is always truthy. Use Hob-native equivalents (`filter`+`first`, `map`) or pre-compute values for synchronous JS lookups.

## See Also

- [Core Concepts](item://a0a0a0a0-d0c0-4000-8000-000000000002) — Views, view specs, and field views
- [viewport-rendering](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) — View resolution, render instances, and API enrichment
- [Attachments](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) — Contextual view configuration
- [Content Field Conventions](item://c0c0c0c0-0024-0000-0000-000000000000) — `for_type`, `ui_hints` fields
- [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — Two-tier save and selector-filtered reactivity
- [Stale Closure Prevention](item://c0c0c0c0-0011-0000-0000-000000000000) — Fresh data in event handlers
- [Stable Skeleton with Surgical Updates](item://c0c0c0c0-0017-0000-0000-000000000000) — Efficient local-state views
